source.note: Mica (CEO Ample Market) x Anthony — AI agents, MCP workflows, scheduled tasks, and the future of outbound GTM

transcripts.topic: Agentic GTM, MCPs, scheduled tasks, signal-triggered outbound

topics.title: Outbound isn't dead. Templated outbound is.
topics.angle: The 'outbound is dead' take is lazy pattern-matching. What's dead is 'Hey {first_name}, I noticed you're at {company_name}, let's hop on a call.' Contextual, signal-triggered outbound generates more pipeline than ever.
topics.sourceQuote: The part of outbound that is dead is like the templated outbound... 'hey first name, I noticed that you are at company name, let's do a call' — that's dead. Nowadays you can be a lot more sophisticated.

topics.title: The 40/40/20 channel mix is still real
topics.angle: Real data from a sales-engagement platform across all of 2026: 40% of opportunities came from email, 40% from calling, 20% from social. Anyone telling you a single channel is 'dead' is selling you something.
topics.sourceQuote: If you take a look at opportunities generated by channel, it was 40/40/20. So 40% email, 40% calling, and 20% social out of all the opportunities.

topics.title: The AI-in-GTM delta is shockingly small (and that's the opportunity)
topics.angle: Engineering work was fundamentally rebuilt in 12 months. Sales work barely changed. Most sellers are using Claude/ChatGPT in a tab next to their CRM — they were already doing that. The teams that close this delta first eat everyone else.
topics.sourceQuote: If you look back like twelve months ago, the way that engineers were working was fundamentally different than the way they're actually working today. And if you look back twelve months ago, the way that sales teams work was not so different than the way that they are working today.

topics.title: Skills users are the top 1%. Scheduled-task users are the top 0.1%.
topics.angle: A simple proxy for AI fluency on a GTM team: ask who's using Claude/ChatGPT skills, then ask who's using scheduled tasks. If you're running scheduled agents that wake up before you do, you're in the top 0.1% of operators.
topics.sourceQuote: If you're using skills, you're top one percent AI users. If you're using scheduled tasks, then you are top point one percent AI users.

topics.title: Two agents, working in tandem, is the new sequence
topics.angle: The pattern that actually works: one agent on the web doing research and enrichment, a second agent inside your engagement platform that owns your channels and CRM context. They talk to each other. The rep approves or rejects. That's the whole stack.
topics.sourceQuote: It's almost like you have two agents here communicating. And every single one of these little things here, as you can see, is a different agent... two agents working in tandem for you and either taking actions on behalf of you or suggesting actions that you should undertake.

topics.title: The four Lego pieces of an agentic GTM stack
topics.angle: Every agentic GTM workflow is some combination of four building blocks: a smart intern (LLM), web search, a data platform, and a workflows/engagement agent. Once you see the four pieces, you can build any play.
topics.sourceQuote: If you have the building blocks that look like this, you have like a really smart intern. You have like web search... I'm assuming that you have like a data platform and a workflows agent.

topics.title: The Lego store problem in AI adoption
topics.angle: Most GTM leaders aren't blocked on tooling. They're blocked on imagination. You handed them 200 Lego pieces. They're staring at the table. The work is whiteboarding which recurring tasks deserve an agent — not buying another platform.
topics.sourceQuote: When you go to the Lego store, you have these sets of Lego pieces that are already... you know you want the Batman Lego piece. But I could tell you, here's a hundred yellow Lego pieces and a hundred black Lego pieces, but you wouldn't know what to do.

topics.title: The 'very smart intern' test for every recurring task
topics.angle: Before you wire up any agent, run this thought experiment: if I had a very smart intern who could read, search, and act, what would I have them do every day? That list is your agent backlog. Build top-down from there.
topics.sourceQuote: The important thing is just sit down with a white paper and try to understand and think about the things that you do on a daily basis that you wish — if you had a very smart intern that you just hired, what would you ask them to do?

topics.title: Reps will never leave Claude and ChatGPT
topics.angle: Every B2B platform is trying to build 'AI inside our app' so reps work in their UI. They will lose. Reps will always pick the maximally intelligent agent — whichever model is best this week — and call your platform from inside it.
topics.sourceQuote: I see some companies kind of like wanting to feed their own agents within their platforms... having the reps move out of Claude or ChatGPT. And I don't think that's going to happen. I think reps will always want to maximize the intelligence of their agents.

topics.title: Personalization isn't a token — it's the choice of channel
topics.angle: Real personalization isn't swapping {first_name}. It's the agent deciding whether to email, call, or LinkedIn-message this person based on prior history. Same content can be wrong simply because it shipped in the wrong channel.
topics.sourceQuote: Not only the content of every single step is truly bespoke and unique, but even the where you're taking actions is unique... if I cold called them in the past, I kind of like want to start with a cold call, or with a text message if I have their phone number.

topics.title: From a stale conference photo to an enriched, contacted list
topics.angle: Take a picture of a logo wall at a conference. Hand it to an agent. It infers companies from logos, finds the VP Sales / CRO at each, validates emails, and drops them in a sequenced list. The 'list-building' job has collapsed into one prompt.
topics.sourceQuote: Going from a stale picture into something that is like context and contact rich... we have this other thing that allows us — we take pictures of badges and then I say, connect with this person, and then Ample Market infers first name, last name, company name, finds the person and connects automatically.

topics.title: The 3:58 AM agent
topics.angle: Set up one scheduled task that wakes up at 3:58 AM every weekday, scrapes Product Hunt launches from the prior 24 hours, qualifies for B2B fit, enriches the founders, and drops them in your sequencing tool. You start your day at the bottom of a funnel that built itself.
topics.sourceQuote: I have one that is actually monitoring Product Hunt launches... this one runs every Wednesday at 3:58 a.m. — very specific time... by the time I wake up, I now have a list of all of these Product Hunt launches.

topics.title: Stop scheduling list-building. Schedule the next action.
topics.angle: Most teams use AI for the easy half: build me a list. The leverage is in the second half: route the right person to the right owner, suggest the right opening message, draft the channel-appropriate first touch. List-building without action is busywork in a nicer wrapper.
topics.sourceQuote: I see a lot of people using platforms to just do list building... once you have a really good data MCP connected to like your ChatGPT or your Claude... list building can be very sophisticated. But to your point, how do you go from here now to an action?

topics.title: The productivity inequity gap is widening, not closing
topics.angle: The honest story of AI right now isn't a job apocalypse — it's a productivity inequity. The curious, A+ players who've internalized agents are leaving everyone else in the dust. The gap compounds week over week.
topics.sourceQuote: The very curious and A-plus players, the people who are seeing this and taking advantage of it and running with it, they're already leaving people in the dust. And I think that's gonna expand. It's almost going to be this inequity of productivity.

topics.title: Why nobody got fired (and never will)
topics.angle: We added AI across LeanScale delivery, ops, and account health. Margins barely moved. Headcount didn't drop. The reason: the market resets expectations as fast as you ship gains. You ran faster so your competitors don't lap you. There is no rest version.
topics.sourceQuote: We didn't fire anyone. We just increased the quality that we're delivering... the market just normalized everything. And now just people expect more.

topics.title: What we actually do with AI at LeanScale (concrete)
topics.angle: Three real plays: pre-sale diagnostic that compares the prospect's CRM to an ideal-state benchmark, transcript-to-PM auto-routing for every architect call, and agentic monitors on account health. Architects only do meetings now. Quality went up. Nobody got fired.
topics.sourceQuote: Before we even engage with a customer, we're leveraging the transcripts as well as a connection to their CRM to do a full diagnostic... we also leveraging the transcripts to do all the PM work... we have agents monitoring the health of our accounts.

topics.title: The MCP is the moat. The UI is not.
topics.angle: Every GTM tool will eventually have a chat interface. The differentiator is the MCP underneath — does it carry channel ownership, CRM context, and engagement history into the agent's prompt? Without that, the chatbot writes generic outbound dressed up as 'AI.'
topics.sourceQuote: Ample Market being directly connected into your CRM, we have maximum context about what's happening in your CRM. We own all of your channels of communication and we have a really strong and powerful database for business data. And so everything that you want to build as a GTM or a salesperson becomes so much easier.

topics.title: Telegram-to-Hermes-to-CRM: the new follow-up
topics.angle: After an in-person meeting, text your agent on Telegram: 'I just met Anthony — send a connection request and start the intro sequence.' By the time you're in the car, the agent has talked to your engagement platform and the touch is out. No CRM tab opened.
topics.sourceQuote: I have like a little Telegram Hermes running... I would actually say, like, you know, I just met with this person, can you actually send Anthony like a connection request on my behalf? We can actually then like I usually call that like a follow-up post meeting.

topics.title: Signal-triggered Slack-to-multithread
topics.angle: Wire one agent to your inbound Slack channel. When a CRO-level title hits, it cross-references CRM, scores deal value, and suggests a multi-thread connection request to two more senior stakeholders at the account. Three lines of context the rep would have missed.
topics.sourceQuote: I have another one that is monitoring Slack for the most important deals coming in and it's actually suggesting that I actually multi-thread with C-level leadership within those accounts.

topics.title: Two-thirds of every B2B job is the integration tax
topics.angle: Cold list to enriched list to sequencer used to be three platforms, two CSV exports, and a Tuesday. With one MCP-connected prompt, the integration tax disappears. The remaining work — judgment, taste, strategy — is the part you couldn't automate anyway.
topics.sourceQuote: Normally you would go like find the list, then you would try to get the list into another platform and then enrich it, and then take the enriched list and go put it into a sequencing platform. There is enough friction in there to where you probably won't even run some of these plays.

topics.title: Taste is the bottleneck (and you can't AI your way to it)
topics.angle: The smartest intern in the world still needs to be told what good looks like. AI doesn't generate taste — it amplifies it. The leaders that win in 2026 are the ones whose taste compounds faster than their competitor's compute does.
topics.sourceQuote: You still need to have taste of what good is. And I think you can't fully AI your way to doing that... you still need to tell them what to do. And you still have to have a good idea and a good vision.

topics.title: The 5-step Product Hunt agent — annotated
topics.angle: Scrape yesterday's launches. Qualify for B2B fit. Extract 4 unique launch insights per company. Push enriched contacts to a list in your engagement platform. Drop them into a named outreach agent. Five steps. Runs autonomously every day at 3:58 AM.
topics.sourceQuote: The steps is like scrape yesterday's Product Hunt launches, qualify each launch for B2B fit, so I don't care about all of them, I just care about B2B, extract four unique launch insights, you put them into dynamic fields, you send those into a list within Ample Market, and then you feed them into the Ample Market agent.

topics.title: If you don't know how to use AI, ask AI
topics.angle: The most underrated unblock for non-technical operators: stop reading agent tutorials. Open Claude. Type 'I want an agent that runs every weekday at 6 AM and pulls X from Y.' The model will walk you through what it needs to know. The instructions write themselves.
topics.sourceQuote: This feels complicated, but it is not. It's actually very simple and Claude can help you do this very easily... If you don't know how to use AI, just ask AI. It's going to give you step-by-step instructions of what you need to do and it'll help you build whatever you want to build.

topics.title: Routing > generation
topics.angle: The unsexy half of agentic GTM is routing. Your enrichment agent found 80 contacts. Some belong to you. Some belong to your AE. Some belong to nobody yet. The play isn't 'enrich faster' — it's 'route to the right human with the right next-best-action.'
topics.sourceQuote: Now you have the podcast, you're able to find the people, you're able to infer who the people are, and you're able to get their contact information. But then like what you want to do with this is potentially you wanna take the next step... map this across your own CRM. Some of these accounts might be owned by me, some by my team.

topics.title: Approve-or-reject is the right interaction model
topics.angle: Full autonomy is the wrong default for revenue-facing AI. The interface that actually works: agent surfaces a recommended action with full context, human says yes/no, agent executes. Trust scales with reps reviewing 100 actions a day, not 10.
topics.sourceQuote: You can say, hey, yeah, run it fully autonomously if you want to. There's checkpoints you can have where you keep the human in the loop... For me, every day I come here and all I do is I say yes or no to these tasks.

topics.title: Hiring is accelerating, not collapsing — at least in SDR
topics.angle: Loud narrative: AI is replacing SDRs. Quiet reality from operators running AI-native sales orgs: SDR teams are the fastest-growing function. Customers are doubling and tripling their BDR/AE counts. Volume of pipeline opportunity is going up, not down.
topics.sourceQuote: My fastest growing team has been my SDR team... I see that in customers too. I see customers doubling, tripling their BDR and their AE orgs.

topics.title: Context-rich beats high-volume every time
topics.angle: The good outbound message has 3-5 pieces of provable context the prospect didn't expect you to know. Closed-lost reason from 4 months ago. Mutual connection. A signal from their stack. Volume is a tax on attention; context is a gift.
topics.sourceQuote: Take a look at the CRM AE mantra, SDR mantra, or past close lost reason and feed that into your creation of the story... there's a who that comes with a specific reason. We propagate that who with the why. Why is Chris here?

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The productivity inequity gap is widening, not closing
posts.postText:
**The honest story of AI right now isn't a job apocalypse — it's a productivity inequity.**

The curious, A+ players who've internalized agents are leaving everyone else in the dust.

Not 2x. Not 3x. Compounding week over week.

This isn't theory — it's what we're watching unfold across every GTM team we work with. The ones who lean in are pulling away. The ones who wait are falling further behind every sprint.

Swipe through 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded thesis hook with 'isn't just X — it's Y' construction. Uses 'This isn't theory' sample phrasing. Pointing-down emoji CTA matches his canonical close. Medium-length, scannable line breaks.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel telling the story of AI's compounding productivity gap between curious operators and everyone else — cover thesis, stat on the gap, 3-pattern framework on who pulls ahead, listicle of behaviors A+ players share, dark closing CTA on what to do about it.

params.title: The AI gap isn't closing. It's widening.
params.body: The curious are leaving everyone else in the dust — and it compounds every week.

params.title: Every week, the gap reopens
params.statLabel: Time for the gap to widen again
params.body: Every week the A+ players ship another agent, another workflow, another shortcut. The middle stays still.

params.title: The three traits we see in every AI A+ player
params.body: Curiosity over comfort. Building over consuming. Shipping over studying. Same three traits, across every team that's leaving the pack behind.

params.title: Five behaviors A+ players share
items: They build before they're told to
They share prompts inside the team Slack
They rebuild last month's workflow every month
They treat agents as teammates, not tools
They expect 10x and get annoyed at 2x

params.title: Pick a side. The middle is dissolving.
params.body: There's no neutral position on AI fluency anymore. Either you're compounding or you're being lapped.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: The 40/40/20 channel mix is still real
posts.postText:
**Anyone telling you a channel is 'dead' is selling you something.**

Real data from a sales-engagement platform, all of 2026, every opportunity generated:

40% email. 40% calling. 20% social.

Not a tilt. Not a death. A mix.

The playbook hasn't changed — the execution has. Email is still email, calling is still calling, social is still social. The teams winning aren't the ones picking one channel. They're the ones running all three with discipline.

Save this for the next time someone tries to sell you a single-channel religion 👇
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded contrarian thesis up top, hard numbers in the body, sample phrasing 'selling you something' lifted from his observed style. Closes with save-the-image CTA and pointing-down emoji — his canonical formatting.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single 1080x1080 stat infographic showing the 40/40/20 opportunity-by-channel breakdown across an entire year of platform data, with the thesis that no channel is dead.

params.title: Every channel is still pulling its weight
params.body: Full-year sales-engagement platform data. Every opportunity generated. The mix is alive — only single-channel religion is dead.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Why nobody got fired (and never will)
posts.postText:
**We added AI across delivery, ops, and account health at LeanScale. Margins barely moved. Headcount didn't drop.**

Why?

The market resets expectations as fast as you ship gains.

We didn't fire anyone. We just raised the quality bar — faster diagnostics, deeper analysis, sharper recommendations. And then the market normalized everything. Now clients expect what AI made possible as the new baseline.

You don't get to keep the surplus. You run faster so your competitors don't lap you.

There is no rest version of this.
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded thesis, 'Why?' pivot, em-dash asides — all canonical Anthony. Uses LeanScale as the proof point (his case-study pattern). Closes on a hard payoff line, not a CTA. No emoji here to vary from other posts.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Outbound isn't dead. Templated outbound is.
posts.postText:
**'Outbound is dead' is lazy pattern-matching.**

What's dead is: "Hey {first_name}, I noticed you're at {company_name}, let's hop on a call."

What's not dead — and is generating more pipeline than ever — is contextual, signal-triggered outbound:

- The prospect's company just raised a Series B
- They posted about a hiring problem on LinkedIn last week
- Their CRO came from a customer of yours
- A tool they depend on just had an outage

The sophistication floor moved. The channel didn't die — the lazy version did.

If your team is still running 2019 templates and blaming the channel, the channel isn't the problem.
posts.voiceNotes: Contrarian-thesis hook in bold, then a fast pivot to specifics. Bullets are scenario-flavored — matches his pattern of using bullets for concrete examples not feature lists. Clean payoff landing, no emoji, no CTA.

posts.author: Anthony Enrico
posts.topicTitle: Reps will never leave Claude and ChatGPT
posts.postText:
**Every B2B platform is racing to build "AI inside our app" so reps work in their UI.**

They will lose.

Reps will always pick the maximally intelligent agent — whichever model is best this week — and call your platform from inside it. Not the other way around.

The pattern I keep seeing: vendors trying to pull reps out of Claude or ChatGPT and into their own native agents. The math doesn't work. Your in-app agent is locked to one model, one update cycle, one roadmap. Claude and ChatGPT are gaining capability every few weeks.

The winning play isn't to be the agent. It's to be the system of record the agent calls. Open the APIs. Build the MCP server. Let the rep stay where they're already 3x productive.

The platform that fights this loses the rep.
posts.voiceNotes: Bolded category thesis — Anthony has earned the founder POV posture per the prompt notes. Em-dashes and 'every few weeks' cadence match his observed style. Lands on a hard 'wins/loses' equation, no emoji, no sign-off — clean payoff to vary from the carousel and infographic closes.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: The four Lego pieces of an agentic GTM stack
posts.postText:
Every agentic GTM workflow we've built at LeanScale is some combination of the same four building blocks.

Once you can name them, you stop being intimidated by "AI agents" and start shipping plays.

Swipe through the four pieces and the one mental model we use to wire them together.

#revops #aiagents
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with operator-to-operator framing ("we've built at LeanScale"), uses his short declarative cadence, lands with a 2-tag hashtag bundle — one of his canonical closers. Avoids contrarian-bait. No emoji on this one to save the salute for elsewhere.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide swipe deck. Cover names the framework. Four body slides each name one building block (LLM / web search / data platform / workflows agent) with a one-line role description. Closing dark slide ties them together with the LeanScale wordmark.

params.title: The four Lego pieces of any AI play
params.body: Once you can name the pieces, you can build any agentic workflow your team needs.

params.title: A smart intern
params.body: An LLM that can read context, reason, and write — the reasoning layer every other piece plugs into.

params.title: Web search
params.body: The agent's view of the outside world — fresh signals on companies, people, news, funding.

params.title: A data platform
params.body: Your CRM and warehouse — the system of record the agent reads from and writes back to.

params.title: A workflows agent
params.body: The engagement layer — what actually sends the email, posts in Slack, updates the deal, books the meeting.

params.title: Snap the four pieces together. That's the play.
params.body: Every agentic GTM workflow we ship is some mix of these four. Nothing more exotic.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: What we actually do with AI at LeanScale (concrete)
posts.postText:
Three AI plays we run internally at LeanScale every day:

1. Pre-sale diagnostic — we connect to the prospect's CRM and run their config against an ideal-state benchmark before we ever take a call.
2. Transcript-to-PM auto-routing — every architect call gets transcribed, parsed, and routed into PM work without a human PM.
3. Agentic account-health monitors — agents watch our account signals and flag risk before our team sees it.

Our architects only do meetings now. Quality went up. Nobody got fired.

🫡
posts.voiceNotes: Listicle-by-construction with bolded-style item names + em-dash descriptions — his bread and butter. Front-loads the concrete plays. Closes with the salute emoji (used here so it doesn't appear on every post). Steady operator register, no buzzwords.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single 1080x1080 graphic — a 3-row listicle showing the three internal AI plays with one-line descriptions. Clean editorial layout, LeanScale wordmark bottom right.

params.title: Three AI plays we run every day
items: Pre-sale diagnostic — CRM config vs. ideal-state benchmark, before the first call.
Transcript-to-PM auto-routing — every architect call becomes PM work, no human PM.
Account-health monitors — agents flag account risk before our team spots it.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Signal-triggered Slack-to-multithread
posts.postText:
A play we wired up internally that I'd recommend any RevOps team copy:

The setup — one agent listens on our inbound Slack channel where new leads land in real time.

When a CRO-, VP Sales-, or COO-level title hits, the agent does three things before anyone on my team reads the message:

1. Cross-references the account against our CRM — open deals, prior conversations, customer status.
2. Scores the deal value based on company stage, headcount, and our ICP fit.
3. Suggests a multi-thread connection request — two more senior stakeholders at that same account, with reasons why.

The rep walks in with three lines of context they would've otherwise had to dig for. The reply goes out in minutes, not hours.

Four building blocks. One agent. Material lift on inbound conversion.
posts.voiceNotes: Classic TACTICAL PLAYBOOK shape — setup + numbered steps + payoff. Uses his RevOps shorthand (ICP, CRM, multi-thread). Lands on a clean operator line instead of a CTA or hashtag — varies the closer.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: Hiring is accelerating, not collapsing — at least in SDR
posts.postText:
The loud narrative: AI is killing the SDR.

The quiet reality from inside AI-native sales orgs: SDR teams are the fastest-growing function.

My fastest-growing team at LeanScale this year? SDRs.

And I'm seeing it in customers too — companies doubling and tripling their BDR and AE headcount, not cutting it.

The reason is mechanical, not philosophical. The volume of pipeline opportunity per rep is going up, because the cost of a touch is going down. More targets become reachable. More plays become viable. The bottleneck moves from "can we send the email" to "can we have the conversation."

That second job is still very much a human job.

#revops #aigtm
posts.voiceNotes: Reads as INDUSTRY OBSERVATION dressed in a sharper hook — Joe doesn't normally do contrarian, but the kernel itself is contrarian against the loud narrative, and the body grounds it in his actual vantage point ("my fastest-growing team"). Pattern-recognition voice + 2-tag hashtag close.

posts.author: Joe Zaghloul
posts.topicTitle: From a stale conference photo to an enriched, contacted list
posts.postText:
At a conference last month I took a picture of the sponsor logo wall.

Thirty minutes later I had a sequenced outbound list.

The workflow — hand the photo to an agent, it infers companies from the logos, finds the VP Sales or CRO at each, validates the email, drops them into Apollo or Ample Market as a working list.

We run a similar play on badge photos — snap a picture of someone's badge, say "connect with this person," and Ample Market infers first name, last name, company, finds the LinkedIn, and sends the connection.

The "list-building" job that used to take an SDR two days has collapsed into one prompt.

The interesting question now isn't whether the list can be built. It's what you do with the time you get back.
posts.voiceNotes: CASE STUDY SNIPPET with a first-person operator hook. Specific tools named (Apollo, Ample Market) — his signature move. Lands on an operator-flavored open question rather than a CTA. No emoji, no hashtags — variety against the other four posts.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Context-rich beats high-volume every time
posts.postText:
The best outbound message I've seen lately had 4 pieces of context the prospect didn't expect us to know.

A closed-lost reason from 4 months ago. A mutual connection. A signal from their stack. A line from a podcast their CRO went on.

That's not a sequence. That's a gift.

Volume is a tax on attention. Context is the opposite.

We put together a few slides on what we mean by context-rich outbound — and how we're using the CRM, AE notes, and old closed-lost reasons as the raw material. Swipe through.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's teacherly downshift — short setup lines, plain operator vocabulary, no contrarian framing. 'Volume is a tax / context is a gift' lands clean without sounding hot-take. Soft swipe CTA matches his linked-resource style.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel: cover stating the thesis, framework slide on the 4 pieces of context, listicle on where to mine that context inside the CRM, stat slide showing the multiplier on reply rate, dark closing slide with the payoff line.

params.title: Context-rich beats high-volume every time
params.body: The best cold messages carry 3-5 pieces of provable context the prospect didn't expect you to know.

params.title: The raw materials of a real outbound
params.body: A closed-lost reason. A mutual connection. A signal from their stack. A line from something their leadership said publicly.

params.title: Already in your CRM, if you look
items: AE notes from the last call cycle
Closed-lost reasons from the prior 6 months
SDR mantra fields and pain tags
Past champion movement and title changes
Mutual connections surfaced by LinkedIn

params.title: The sweet spot for proof of context
params.body: Anything less reads like a template. Anything more reads like surveillance. Three to five is the sweet spot.

params.title: Volume is a tax. Context is a gift.
params.body: Stop sending more. Start sending messages that prove you actually know who this person is and why they're worth your time.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Routing > generation
posts.postText:
The unsexy half of agentic GTM is routing.

Your enrichment agent just found 80 contacts. Great. Some of them belong to you. Some belong to your AE. Some belong to nobody yet.

The play isn't 'enrich faster.' The play is 'route to the right human with the right next-best-action.'

That's where most agent builds quietly stall — the generation step gets all the love and the routing step gets none of it. Put it on the same level as the prompt and the workflow gets real.

One visual on how we're thinking about it.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's calm-instructive register — names the unloved part of the stack and gives it credit. No hot-take framing, no profanity. Closes with 'one visual on how we're thinking about it' which matches his light, resource-pointing closer.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single 1080x1080 framework graphic with three lanes for the routing decision: contact belongs to me, belongs to my AE, belongs to nobody yet — each with the matching next-best-action.

params.title: Three lanes every enrichment agent has to decide between
params.body: Mine (recycle in my cadence). My AE's (warm hand-off with the context). Nobody's yet (drop into the right rep's territory with a next-best-action). Generation is the easy part. Routing is the unlock.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: The 'very smart intern' test for every recurring task
posts.postText:
Before you wire up any agent, run this thought experiment.

If I had a very smart intern — someone who could read, search, and act — what would I have them do every day?

That list is your agent backlog. Build top-down from there.

Here's how I'd actually run it:

1. Open a blank doc. No tools, no platforms, just the doc.
2. List every recurring task you touch in a normal week. Account research. Pipeline hygiene. Meeting prep. Lost-deal review.
3. Star the ones you wish a smart, careful person would just do for you.
4. Write the one-paragraph instruction you'd give that intern for each starred item.
5. THOSE are your first agents. In that order.

Most teams skip step 1 and 2 and go straight to picking a platform. That's why their agents feel like demos and not like leverage.

Start with the list. The build gets easy once the list is honest.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron in teacherly TACTICAL PLAYBOOK mode — numbered steps, plain operator language, no emoji. Opens with the thought experiment line lifted from his actual phrasing. Closes with a clean payoff, not a CTA.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: Personalization isn't a token — it's the choice of channel
posts.postText:
There's a misunderstanding about personalization in outbound I keep running into.

Most people think personalization is the content of the message — swapping {first_name}, name-dropping a podcast, referencing the funding round.

That's table stakes now. The real personalization is the channel.

The right agent decides whether to email, call, or LinkedIn-message this specific person based on what's worked with them before. Same exact content can be wrong simply because it shipped in the wrong place.

If I cold called you last quarter and you picked up, the next touch should probably be another call — not an email.
If you replied on LinkedIn, that's where I'm coming back to you.
If I texted you and you wrote back, the cold email looks lazy by comparison.

The channel choice carries half the signal. Make sure your agents are making that call deliberately, not by default.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron's SOFT EDUCATIONAL voice — names the confusion, teaches the cleaner model, lands with a useful mental shift. Lifts the channel-choice framing from his own phrasing. Avoids contrarian opening; uses 'a misunderstanding I keep running into' instead.

posts.author: Cameron Legge
posts.topicTitle: If you don't know how to use AI, ask AI
posts.postText:
Pattern I'm seeing across the operators on our team and the customer teams we work with right now.

The non-technical folks who are building real agents have stopped reading tutorials. They open Claude and they type something like:

"I want an agent that runs every weekday at 6 AM and pulls X from Y and drops a summary in my inbox."

Then they let the model walk them through what it needs to know. The instructions write themselves. The build feels approachable instead of intimidating.

This is the part I wish more GTM operators heard.

It feels complicated. It is not. If you don't know how to use AI, just ask AI. It will give you step-by-step instructions for whatever you're trying to build, and you'll get further in an afternoon than a week of YouTube tutorials.

The operators who internalize this in the next 6 months are going to look very different from the ones who don't.
posts.voiceNotes: Cameron INDUSTRY OBSERVATION mode — opens with 'pattern I'm seeing,' uses his calm teacherly closer, no contrarian framing. Lifts 'if you don't know how to use AI, just ask AI' verbatim because it's the actual quote and reads like him.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The Lego store problem in AI adoption
posts.postText:
Most GTM leaders aren't blocked on AI tooling. They're blocked on imagination.

We handed them 200 Lego pieces and they're still staring at the table.

When you walk into the Lego store, you don't buy a bin of yellow bricks and a bin of black bricks and figure it out at home. You buy the Batman set. The instructions are the product.

That's what most AI rollouts are missing — not a better platform, not another seat license. A picture of what to build.

The real work right now isn't procurement. It's whiteboarding which recurring tasks in your week actually deserve an agent — and then specifying them well enough that someone could build them.

Swipe through. This is the conversation we're having with every GTM leader in the portfolio.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with a reframe (not a stat) — pattern-recognition voice from being in the room. Uses the Lego analogy as the through-line. Closes with a clean declarative landing and a soft swipe CTA — no emoji, no 'thoughts?' bait.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: 5-slide carousel walking through the Lego analogy. Cover frames the problem (imagination gap, not tooling gap). Middle slides contrast the two ways to buy Lego — bricks vs sets — and map that to AI adoption. A listicle slide names the four recurring tasks worth specifying first. Dark closer lands the thesis.

params.title: Most AI rollouts fail at the table, not the store
params.body: GTM teams aren't blocked on tooling. They're blocked on imagination.

params.title: Bricks vs sets
params.body: Bricks: 200 yellow, 200 black, figure it out. Sets: a picture, instructions, a finished thing. Guess which one gets built.

params.title: Specification beats procurement
params.body: Buying another platform doesn't tell your team what to build. Naming the recurring task does.

params.title: Four recurring tasks worth specifying first
items: Inbound lead enrichment and routing within five minutes of form fill
Deal review prep — pulling the last 30 days of activity into a one-pager
Renewal risk scoring against your agreed-upon health definition
Weekly competitor and account-news digest for named accounts

params.title: Stop buying bricks. Draw the set.
params.body: The teams pulling away in 2026 aren't the ones with more seats. They're the ones who whiteboarded what to build first.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Two-thirds of every B2B job is the integration tax
posts.postText:
Most B2B work isn't the work. It's the tax on the work.

The old version of building a play: find a list, get it into another platform, enrich it, pull the enriched file out, push it into a sequencer. Three platforms, two CSV exports, and a Tuesday.

That friction is the reason most teams never actually run the play.

With one MCP-connected prompt, the integration tax goes to zero. What's left is the work you actually wanted to do — picking the right segment, writing the right message, deciding what good looks like.

The judgment, the taste, the strategy. The part you couldn't automate anyway.

The interesting thing isn't that AI got better. It's that AI finally killed the part of the job nobody ever wanted.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry-observation register with a clean reframe in the opener. Specifics over abstractions — names the exact old workflow vs the new one. Lands on the substance, no CTA, no emoji. Matches his 'predictably good when structured' rhythm.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single-slide infographic with a stat callout. Big number front and center showing the share of B2B work that's integration plumbing vs actual judgment. Short label clarifies what the number represents, body line lands the takeaway.

params.title: Most of the job was never the job
params.statLabel: of a B2B workflow used to be moving data between tools
params.body: One MCP-connected prompt kills the plumbing. What's left is judgment, taste, and strategy — the part you couldn't automate anyway.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: The AI-in-GTM delta is shockingly small (and that's the opportunity)
posts.postText:
Engineering work was fundamentally rebuilt in the last 12 months. Sales work barely changed.

Watch a dev today vs a dev a year ago — different job. Watch an AE today vs an AE a year ago — same dashboard, same sequence, same Tuesday.

Most sellers I talk to are using Claude or ChatGPT in a tab next to their CRM. They were already doing that.

The delta between how AI rebuilt engineering and how AI rebuilt sales is shockingly small. Which means it's coming, fast, and the teams that close it first eat everyone else.

The window to be early on this is measured in quarters, not years.
posts.voiceNotes: Stat-as-hook reframe in his signature style — leads with a sharp comparative claim, immediately reframes. Parallel structure ('Watch a dev... watch an AE...'). Clean declarative landing, no question bait, no emoji.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Taste is the bottleneck (and you can't AI your way to it)
posts.postText:
Pattern across the GTM leaders I've talked to this quarter:

The ones pulling away aren't the ones with the most agents. They're the ones with the clearest definition of what good looks like.

AI is the smartest intern in the world. The smartest intern in the world still needs to be told what to write, what to ship, what to escalate, what to leave alone.

A few things I keep seeing:

→ Teams without a written definition of a good outbound message generate worse outbound with AI than they did without it.
→ Teams without an agreed-upon deal-stage rubric get AI deal scoring that no rep trusts.
→ Teams without a sharp ICP statement watch their enrichment costs balloon enriching the wrong companies faster.

AI doesn't generate taste. It amplifies whatever taste — or lack of it — is already in the system.

The leaders that win in 2026 are the ones whose taste compounds faster than their competitor's compute does.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry-observation hook ('pattern across the leaders I've talked to') — his behind-the-scenes anchor style. Uses three parallel bullets with the → arrow he reaches for. Lands on a clean declarative thesis, no CTA, no emoji bundle.

posts.author: Yaseen Arshad
posts.topicTitle: Skills users are the top 1%. Scheduled-task users are the top 0.1%.
posts.postText:
A simple proxy for AI fluency on a GTM team — two questions, in order:

1. Who on your team is using Claude or ChatGPT skills?
2. Who is using scheduled tasks?

If you can name two or three people on question one, you're in the top 1% of operators. Most teams can't name anyone.

If you can name even one person on question two, you're in the top 0.1%.

The difference between the two questions is the difference between using AI as a faster search box and using AI as a member of your team. A scheduled task is an agent that wakes up before you do, runs the play, and leaves the output on your desk.

The ceiling on what one operator can produce went up by an order of magnitude this year. Most people are still working at last year's ceiling because they haven't moved past question one.

Go ask your team both questions. The answers will tell you exactly where the leverage is hiding.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft-educational concept teach — introduces a clean two-question diagnostic. Lifts his 'big rocks' / operator-mindset register. Closes with a tactical instruction rather than a CTA. Different opener (numbered diagnostic), different closer (instruction) from the other four posts in the batch.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The 5-step Product Hunt agent — annotated
posts.postText:
Built a scheduled agent inside LeanScale Labs that runs every weekday at 3:58 AM.

By the time I'm up, I have a list of yesterday's Product Hunt launches, qualified for B2B fit, enriched with four launch-specific insights per company, pushed into a named list in Ample Market, and queued inside an outreach agent waiting on my approve/reject.

Five steps. Zero manual touches before coffee.

Swipe through for the annotated walkthrough — each slide is one step, what tool runs it, and why that step exists.

If you want the workflow JSON or the prompt for the insight extractor, drop a comment.
posts.voiceNotes: Opens with the artifact (a shipped scheduled agent) and the specific 3:58 AM detail — matches Jake's show-don't-shout hook style. Uses concrete tool names (Ample Market, LeanScale Labs). Closes with the practitioner CTA from his sample phrasings ('the workflow JSON').
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Six-slide carousel walking through a scheduled Product Hunt enrichment agent: cover, then one slide per step (scrape, qualify, extract insights, push to engagement list, hand off to outreach agent), closing with a dark recap slide on the two-agent pattern.

params.title: The 5-step Product Hunt agent
params.body: One scheduled task. Runs at 3:58 AM. Hands me a qualified, enriched outreach list before I'm awake.

params.title: Scrape yesterday's launches
params.body: Pulls every Product Hunt launch from the prior 24 hours. Raw feed in, structured rows out.

params.title: Qualify for B2B fit
params.body: Filter against a B2B rubric. Consumer launches drop. Only fits move forward — I don't want the rest.

params.title: Extract 4 launch insights
params.body: Per company: what they shipped, who it's for, the angle, the tie-in. Lands in dynamic fields for personalization.

params.title: Push to Ample Market list
params.body: Enriched contacts land in a named list inside the engagement platform. CRM context already attached.

params.title: Hand off to the outreach agent
params.body: Sequences are queued, not sent. I approve or reject in the morning. The agent does the rest.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Two agents, working in tandem, is the new sequence
posts.postText:
The pattern I keep landing on when we build outbound for clients in LeanScale Labs:

One agent lives on the web. It does research, scrapes, enriches, qualifies.

A second agent lives inside the engagement platform. It owns the channels, the CRM context, and the sequence state.

The two talk to each other. The rep approves or rejects. That's the whole stack — and it replaces a lot of what we used to call 'a sequence.'

One image below mapping the handoff. Save it if you're rebuilding your outbound stack this quarter.
posts.voiceNotes: Earnest builder explainer — 'the pattern I keep landing on' fits his observed phrasings. Names the layers concretely (research/enrichment vs. channels/CRM). Closing line is a soft practitioner CTA ('save it'), not a sales ask.
posts.visualAssetNeeded: Single infographic showing two distinct agents connected by a handoff arrow. Left agent labeled with web research and enrichment actions. Right agent labeled with channel ownership and CRM context. A 'rep approves/rejects' checkpoint sits between them.

params.title: Two agents, working in tandem
items: Agent 1 — lives on the web. Scrapes, enriches, qualifies.
Agent 2 — lives in the engagement platform. Owns channels + CRM.
They pass context to each other on every record.
Rep sits in the middle. Approves or rejects each action.
That's the stack. No more linear, hand-built sequences.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The MCP is the moat. The UI is not.
posts.postText:
Every AI-native GTM tool we're evaluating with clients ships the same surface feature now: a chat interface.

Within 12 months, every GTM tool will have one. The chat box is not the moat.

The moat is the MCP underneath it. Does it carry channel ownership into the prompt? CRM context? Live engagement history? Account state?

Without that, the chatbot writes generic outbound dressed up as 'AI.' With it, the same prompt produces something a rep would actually send.

When I look at Ample Market — direct CRM connection, ownership of the comms channels, a real business-data layer underneath — that's the part that compounds. The chat is just the surface.

If you're picking AI tools right now, ignore the demo video. Read the integration docs.
posts.voiceNotes: Industry observation framing ('pattern I'm seeing') from his sample phrasings. Avoids contrarian hook in favor of pattern-recognition voice. Concrete tool names. Closing line is practitioner advice, not a CTA — varies the sign-off.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: Approve-or-reject is the right interaction model
posts.postText:
Full autonomy is the wrong default for any AI that touches revenue.

The interaction model that's actually working in the agents we've shipped at LeanScale Labs is simpler: the agent surfaces a recommended action with full context. The human says yes or no. The agent executes.

The checkpoint is the feature, not a limitation.

A rep can review 100 of these a day. They cannot review 10 sent emails after the fact and unwind them. Trust scales with reversibility — and approve-or-reject keeps every action reversible until the moment it ships.

For me, this is what the morning looks like now. I open the queue, I read the recommended action and the reasoning, I click yes or no. The agent runs the play. I get to spend the rest of my day on the work the agent can't do.

That's the interface. Not full autonomy. Not co-pilot. Approve-or-reject.
posts.voiceNotes: Soft educational explainer of a concept (the approve/reject interaction model). Plain-spoken, declarative, no hype. First-person grounded in his actual workflow. No emoji, no sign-off stamp — closes with the new mental model in the reader's pocket, per the SOFT EDUCATIONAL spec.

posts.author: Jake Toepel
posts.topicTitle: The 3:58 AM agent
posts.postText:
If you want a Product Hunt outreach motion that builds itself overnight, here's the build order we use:

1. Set up a scheduled task in your agent platform. Wake it at 3:58 AM every weekday. Specific time matters — don't pile it on the 4 AM cron with everything else.

2. Scrape the prior 24 hours of Product Hunt launches. JSON in, structured rows out.

3. Run each row through a B2B qualifier prompt. Drop the consumer stuff. You only want fits.

4. Enrich the founders. LinkedIn, email, role, company stage. Pipe to dynamic fields.

5. Push the qualified, enriched contacts into a named list inside your sequencing tool — Ample Market, Outreach, whichever.

6. Hand off to a named outreach agent that drafts the message using the launch-specific insights you extracted in step 3.

When you wake up, you're not at the top of the funnel. You're at the bottom of one that built itself overnight. All you do is approve or reject.

Happy to share the prompt for the B2B qualifier — comment 'PH' and I'll send it.
posts.voiceNotes: Tactical playbook in numbered SOP format — his primary content type per the voice profile. Opens with the problem-statement setup line. Closes with the soft practitioner CTA from sample phrasings ('Happy to share the prompt'). Concrete platform names. No emoji.
